one_line_takeaway: OmClic turns image-scaling camouflage into a one-image-fits-many-input-sizes clean-label poisoning primitive via per-channel multi-objective optimization, cutting both crafting time (up to ~30x) and backdoor budget (Mx) while hitting 100% ASR, though it inherits image-scaling attacks' fragility and is defeated by a cheap intermediate-resize defense.
key_finding: OmClic matches the SOTA's single-target camouflage fidelity while being far faster and, uniquely, covering multiple input sizes with one image: for a 448 source and 114 target the SOTA needs 1893 s vs OmClic's 67 s (up to ~28x, and up to 30x overall). Single-target similarity is essentially identical to SOTA (e.g. Face-448 SSIM 0.744 SOTA vs 0.742 OmClic), degrading gracefully as more targets (k=2,3) are packed in and recovering with a larger 1024 source. The resulting clean-label backdoor reaches 100% ASR across all datasets (equal to the plain dirty-label baseline) with CDA nearly unchanged from the clean model (PubFig CDA 91.6/92.5/95.8% at input sizes 96/112/224). The same poisoned PubFig set transfers to VGG16 and DenseNet121. A 0.61% poison rate (30/4921 PubFig images) still yields 95.4% ASR. The SSBA variant with 10 cover samples gives 97.2% source-class ASR and only 1.7% non-source ASR. The proposed InterResize defense reliably neutralizes the attack (output stays the benign source image).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is OmClic (One-to-Multiple Clean-Label Image Camouflage): the first image-scaling camouflage attack in which a SINGLE crafted attack image simultaneously deceives MULTIPLE model input sizes (M=5 targeted concurrently; up to k=8 target images disguised into one source). It abuses the default image-resize operation of DL frameworks so a benign-looking, correctly-labeled source image (seen by the human curator) collapses into an attacker-chosen, trigger-carrying target image after downscaling (seen by the model). The authors reformulate the crafting problem from the prior single-objective convex optimization of the SOTA (Xiao et al., USENIX 2019, ref [15]) into a MULTI-objective optimization solved via a weighted-sum loss (Eq. 6) using cvxpy, and crucially solve the perturbation PER COLOR CHANNEL (3 invocations) rather than per image line as the SOTA does. This removes the SOTA's frequent optimization failures, cuts crafting time drastically, and reduces the backdoor attack budget (poison rate) by M times because one poisoned image now covers all common input sizes instead of needing one image per size. They also demonstrate OmClic-enabled clean-label backdoors, a source-specific backdoor (SSBA) variant, and propose a lightweight InterResize defense. Note: feature-collision clean-label attacks [10-13] and the original camouflage/image-scaling attack [15,23] are prior/cited work, NOT this paper's contribution; OmClic is the one-to-multiple, per-channel, multi-objective extension plus its backdoor and defense evaluation.
threat_model: Data-outsourcing / clean-label data-poisoning setting. The attacker controls only a small fraction (<0.5% poison rate cited as sufficient) of the training data collected/annotated by a third party, volunteers, or web crawling, and knows the model's input size (a reasonable assumption since common input sizes are few and public, e.g. ResNet 112/224, per Table 1). The attacker has NO control over training. The victim/data curator fully controls training, can visually inspect received images for content-label consistency (and would reject dirty-label poison), but does NOT inspect images AFTER the framework's default resize step. Clean-label camouflage bypasses this human audit because the attack image's visible content matches its label; only after downscaling does the malicious target image emerge. The attack is model-architecture-agnostic (poisoned set transfers across ResNet18/VGG16/DenseNet121) as long as the chosen input size falls within the compromised set.
limitations: By the authors' own account: (1) the attack works only when the victim's chosen input size lies within the pre-targeted set of common input sizes; (2) achieving high attack-image/source-image similarity requires a large source-to-target size ratio (small 448 sources show perceptible artifacts, especially for dissimilar content like the animal set, mitigated by 1024 sources), and artifacts grow with k. Additional constraints evident in the paper: the attacker must know the input size; the camouflage only fires when the SAME resize algorithm (e.g. NEAREST) is used at both crafting and inference time (a NEAREST-vs-LANCZOS mismatch defeats it); the poison rate must be non-trivial (ASR drops as poison images decrease); and the simple InterResize defense the authors themselves introduce, as well as existing detectors (Decamouflage [37]) and hardened resize functions [23], can thwart it. Evaluation is limited to CNNs and a handful of datasets/architectures.

datasets_used: Tiny-ImageNet (10 of 200 classes used, 5,000 train / 500 test, 64x64x3)
Camouflage-quality images (facial, animal, landscape) from wallpaperaccess.com
quantitative_results: Crafting time (source 448, target 114): SOTA [15] 1893 s vs OmClic 67 s (~28x, up to 30x overall)
M=5 input sizes targetable concurrently; up to k=8 target images disguised in one source
Single-target similarity Face-448: SSIM 0.744 (SOTA) vs 0.742 (OmClic); PSNR 27.469 vs 27.483; near-identical
OmClic similarity degrades with more targets, e.g. Face-448 SSIM 0.744->0.565 (2 targets)->0.472 (3 targets); Face-1024 SSIM 0.905 for single target
Backdoor ASR = 100% on all datasets (same as plain dirty-label baseline)
PubFig OmClic CDA = 91.6% / 92.5% / 95.8% at input sizes 96 / 112 / 224
Clean-model baseline accuracies at 224: Caltech256 91.3%, PubFig 95.7%, STL 92.6%, Tiny-ImageNet 89.1%
Plain backdoor CDA at 224: PubFig 95.8%, STL 92.4%, Tiny-ImageNet 89.2%
Poison rate 0.61% (30/4921 PubFig) still yields 95.4% ASR
SSBA with 10 cover samples: source-class ASR 97.2%, non-source-class ASR 1.7%, cover-sample CDA 94.2% (vs clean 95.6%)
Poisoned PubFig set transfers across ResNet18, VGG16, DenseNet121
baselines_compared: Xiao et al. [15] (USENIX Security 2019) single-objective, single-input-size camouflage SOTA - for camouflage fidelity (SSIM/MSSSIM/UQI/PSNR) and crafting time
Plain dirty-label backdoor attack - for CDA and ASR
